column17:
I am an Android engineer at Condé Nast, contributing to the Android app for The New Yorker, a globally recognized brand with millions of users. Prior to this, I worked on the Vogue Runway Android app. Before transitioning to mobile app development, my career began in web development, where I honed my skills in JavaScript, React, CSS, and MongoDB, with Python as my primary programming language. During that time, I also explored data science and machine learning.( Now I don't have hands on it cos I am into mobile app dev, I am confident that I can recall is its need for this cohort)

As an Android engineer, my current focus is Kotlin, and I am deeply committed to delivering high-quality code, intuitive user interfaces, and exceptional user experiences. My work requires close collaboration with designers, product managers, and data analytics teams to ensure our app meets the highest standards.

Working at a product-based company like Condé Nast has exposed me to the dynamic role of product management a field I was unfamiliar with during college. Intrigued by the responsibilities of product managers, I enrolled in a six-month cohort with Growth School to deepen my understanding. Through this program, I learned key concepts like user stories, user flows, RCA, and the importance of launching MVPs to validate hypotheses. I also gained insights into data-driven decision-making and cross-department collaboration. This experience has been invaluable, as it gives me a broader perspective on owning and shaping a product beyond engineering.

Looking ahead, my goal is to develop and scale AI driven products, particularly in generative AI, to millions of users creating both a name and wealth in this evolving industry. I am also passionate about integrating AI tools into my workflow to boost productivity and stay ahead in this rapidly changing field.

I strive to do what others aren’t exploring unique ideas and pushing boundaries. Staying relevant, innovative, and forward-thinking is not just a goal but a necessity for me.
column19:
After completing this cohort, I aim to confidently answer any questions about building Gen AI products and effectively using AI tools. I want to be able to consult businesses on leveraging AI, stay updated with industry trends, and access resources like the 100x Engineer newsletter platform to keep myself ahead in the field.
Currently, the environment around me lacks enthusiasm for upgrading skills or exploring new industries most people stick to conventional paths. As Varun often highlights, competition in fields like full-stack development and UI design is intense, and with AI advancing, those who embrace and use it will thrive. While there’s uncertainty about how the world will evolve over the next five years, one thing is clear: marketing will play a critical role in a product's survival because soon, anyone will be able to build. This reality motivates me to stay a step ahead of the crowd.
Sometimes, I question if it’s all just FOMO similar to the hype around the metaverse and crypto a few years ago but I’m determined to focus on areas with real potential.
My long-term goal is to build and scale applications that generate significant revenue. I envision raising funding when necessary, starting my own venture or company, and potentially exiting after a few years to achieve financial independence. While pursuing these ambitions, I want to connect with people already thriving in the AI industry. For instance, I aspire to work with teams like AEOS Labs to maintain hands-on experience with Gen AI while simultaneously building my business ( Especially with Sridev, Siddhant .
I’m committed to giving my best to this cohort and expect the best outcomes in return. Even if the product idea seems simple or mundane, I want to create something powered by AI that becomes indispensable for people in their daily lives whether it’s chatbots, AI agents, productivity boosters, or solutions that improve quality of life.
I admire apps like Learna AI (Google Play), Replika, and Anima, as well as innovative tools like Umax and WingAI. My vision includes building apps that help users enhance their lives whether through simplifying tasks, improving personal appearance, or boosting overall well-being. Products like these offering skin care advice, styling suggestions, or fitness improvements (might sound funny, but these are the apps that made crazy money, because now on social media everyone wants to look great) such apps resonate deeply with people and have the potential to make a significant impact while generating substantial revenue.
Siddhant and Sridev, I’ve been following your work on the 100x Engineers Instagram page, and without hesitation, I enrolled in this cohort because I’m confident in the quality and ROI you offer. Throughout this journey, I hope you’ll guide me in becoming an AI engineer capable of finding Gen AI job opportunities until my own product matures and becomes a viable source of income.

I am passionate about building AI-driven solutions that revolutionize how businesses operate across multiple domains. With my background in DevOps, cloud infrastructure, and AI-driven automation, I am focusing on the following key areas to create impactful B2B SaaS solutions:

1. AI-Driven Log Analysis & Self-Healing Systems:
Enabling businesses to interact with logs using natural language, extract insights, detect threats, and predict anomalies.
Automating proactive remediation—such as detecting traffic spikes on web servers and automatically scaling infrastructure when needed.

2. Automated Observability Dashboards:-
Developing AI-powered tools that generate Grafana/Kibana dashboards by analyzing sample logs, metrics, and traces.
Reducing the complexity of manual dashboard creation and improving real-time monitoring efficiency.

3. AI-Powered Digital Marketing Optimization:-
Building AI assistants that analyze product data to auto-generate optimized ad campaign parameters for Meta, Google, and YouTube.
Utilizing market trends, customer psychology, and competitor analysis to provide cost-efficient advertising strategies.

4. AI-Driven DevOps Automation
Automating cloud and on-premise architecture design based on client SOPs, business requirements, and infrastructure needs.
Supporting serverless and traditional infrastructures, optimizing CI/CD pipelines, and ensuring seamless environment provisioning.

column47:
Honestly, comparing my whole tech career, I dont think I have built things as interesting or as impressive as my college days hackathon was. 

Back in 2017-2018, as a part of the Smart India Hackathon problem statements from the Department which stated:
- There are a lot of human animal conflict that occur at border areas between forests and villages. How do you propose to address or fix this?
- There are a lot of unwanted forest border crossings by poachers, who sneak in, and kill animals. How do you propose to address or fix this?

We had a really good solution and a small prototype to fix BOTH of these problems.
1. The human-animal conflicts:
- We proposed a two tier solution - The first would be ground systems along the bordering areas that was fitted with a Raspberry pi, multiple PIR sensors to cover a wide area, and night vision cameras. These were responsible to detect movements, capture images, and upload it to our AI servers for processing. The second would be our cloud compute running the CNN model (we used Yolo) that was responsible for identification of the animal. We had trained the model to classify animals as dangerous (Elephant, tigers, lions, etc), and timid (deer, cattle, monkeys, etc). When the Server responds back with a "dangerous" update, our ground systems would trigger an alarm in the localised area, and immediately would prepare a report with the photo, and GPS location of detected intrusion. The alert would give the villagers enough time, to stay safe. And the alerted authorities and police could take swift action with the detailed report.

2. The unwanted poacher crossings:
- We used a similar two tier solution - The on ground raspberry pi system with sensors, cameras, etc. Its job was to capture signs of intrusions, and upload it to the AI server. We had another model running that was trained on facial recognition (of people who are authorized to be in the forests), and it would raise an alert to the local authorities whenever it detected an intruder. (In hindsight, now, I realise we could have extended it to train it on authorized vehicles that may enter through reserved forest paths with nameplat detectetion as well). 

We faced many problems, such as what AI models we ought to use for such tasks, and open source AI was less heard of back then. We didnt call them as AI, we used the terms of Machine learning, Deep learning, convoluted neural networks! We faced problems of energency efficency, of using continuous camera monitoring in low energy availability locations, to adapting the design to be a sensor with photos design to optimize on the energy used. Writing programs to connect the hardware sensors and to build an all round system! One of my firsts! 

As a student, these things were not a part of our syllabus, and I was luckly to learn about these and actually use them to build a solution, solving problems! This! Solving problems. Using tech to solve real world problems! I learnt that I absulutely loved this because of the hackathons I have been in, and that pretty much set the trajectory of how I knew I wanted to be, in tech.

We submitted this and built a working prototype for this project well. We won second place in the Smart India Hackathon, forestry department, and that was surreal! Narrating this story right now, brings back nostalgic memories. I dont think I have felt such a thrill ever since! The sense of acheivement and the sense of awe and wonder and excitement. We were hyped up quite a but with that win, and some officials, wanted to take this project on ground as well, and provide funding. However due to either politics or bureaucracy, we never got the funding, and our college lives took over once again.

Challenge 1: Get notifications in Slack 

One of the challenges while doing outbound for our clients is getting notifications when a prospect replies to our campaigns so my clients can immediately reply.

The problem is that we need to connect the API of our outreach tool, create a webhook in the automation tool, get the right API endpoint, and create a notification message format.

column47:
Project: Generative AI-powered RAG System for Document Analysis  

- Problem: Enterprises spend hundreds of hours manually analyzing large documents for missing data, leading to inefficiencies and errors. The challenge becomes even greater when dealing with multiple document formats (DOC, DOCX, PDFs with OCR, and images requiring OCR processing), where relevant information can appear randomly across different sections of documents.  

- Solution: Built an AI-powered Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) system that automatically extracts, summarizes, and detects missing values across various document formats. Integrated a Streamlit UI for an intuitive user experience and built a unified processing pipeline to handle different input formats seamlessly.  

- Challenges:  
  - Handling large-scale document processing with varying formats (DOC, DOCX, PDF, scanned images with OCR, etc.)  
  - Optimizing for token limits while ensuring high accuracy  
  - Fine-tuning embedding models to retrieve context from different document structures  
  - Randomly located target information across different sections, requiring a single unified pipeline to extract, structure, and process all formats efficiently  

- Success: The tool was recognized at Ford's Asia Pacific awards, significantly reducing manual effort and saving time and costs for document processing.  

- Impact: The solution paved the way for further AI adoption in enterprise workflows, setting a benchmark for future AI-driven tools capable of handling complex, multi-format document analysis with a unified approach.

column47:
Worked on optimising a critical huge SQL code that loads data for Marketing communication to be sent out everyday. It was challenging because the code gets updated every week and pushed into production, so any changes to the code, shouldn't disrupt the BAU processes. The factors considered while optimisation were
1. Most efficient deployment strategy
2. Reduce the risk of data errors and failures as the table is critical for daily marketing communication
3. Best strategy to be used so that it's easy to train 15 people who makes changes to the same code every week. 

Steps taken:
1. Break the code into pieces to analyse the 400 attributes created , their dependencies, source systems etc
2. Come up with the best strategy to split the code so that it can run parallel to reduce the overall runtime.
3. Deployment planned out so that no disruptions to BAU
4. Efficient testing strategies and conducted a session with team members to explain the new workflow
5.  Reduced overall runtime by 20 minutes using the strategy.

I was born and brought up in a small, remote town called Jawhar. Locally referred to as the town of waterfalls, it had a vibrant cultural heritage and was primarily inhabited by a tribal population. However, being a rural town, technological and infrastructure development was negligible. When I got access to the internet in 9th grade, it opened me up to a world of opportunities that were neither accessible nor known to me previously. 
Capitalizing on these new technology-enabled opportunities, I expanded my horizons as I moved across cities for my higher studies, becoming the first person in my family to do so. Just like a fish that keeps jumping into a bigger pond and finally moving into an ocean, I pursued academic and professional opportunities, moved out of my comfort zone, and learned new things that helped me evolve.
While I pursued these new experiences, I simultaneously saw many of my schoolmates end up as daily-wagerers, simply because they weren’t aware of better options, leading me to take up mentorship roles, focused on providing career opportunities to underprivileged students. Taking workshops and 1-on-1 mentoring sessions, I worked with multiple students and helped them get internships and full-time job opportunities - a significant step in improving their quality of life. Similarly, in college, I established an initiative to bring all entrepreneurship cells across universities in Pune City under one roof to foster a collaborative environment and provide mentorship to startups.
When I moved back to Jawhar temporarily during covid, amidst Jawhar’s rich cultural heritage and natural beauty, my friends and I discovered an opportunity for change. The rural area, away from the city’s bustle, was an ideal place for tourism. Together we built camping sites and offered travelers the opportunity to enjoy local food, engage in workshops, and participate in community gatherings. Involving the local community at every step, we hired villagers as tour guides and trainers, creating a huge impact on families who had struggled with financial instability for years by providing an extra source of income.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was building a Customer Churn Prediction Model for a home security company in the USA. The goal was to identify customers who were likely to cancel their subscription so the company could take proactive measures to retain them.


Problem Statement
The home security company operated on a subscription-based model, providing security services and monitoring to homeowners. However, they faced a high churn rate, leading to revenue loss and increased customer acquisition costs.

They wanted a machine learning model to predict churn at least one month in advance so they could take retention actions.
The dataset included customer demographics, contract details, usage patterns, service complaints, and payment behavior.
The company had millions of customers, making it a big data problem.


Challenges & Hurdles Faced

1. Data Collection & Cleaning (Technical Challenge)
The company had data silos across different departments (customer service, billing, CRM).
Data was inconsistent, with missing values and duplicated records.
Solution: We built an ETL pipeline using Python (Pandas, SQLAlchemy) to clean and preprocess the data. We also used feature engineering to create meaningful features, such as:
Time since last complaint
Average monthly support tickets
Payment anomalies (late payments, missed payments)
Contract type (monthly, annual, prepaid, etc.)
2. Imbalanced Dataset (Technical Challenge)
Churn was a rare event (~10% churn rate), making it difficult for the model to learn meaningful patterns.
Solution: We used:
Oversampling (SMOTE) to balance the dataset.
Cost-sensitive learning by adjusting class weights.
AUC-ROC as an evaluation metric instead of accuracy.
3. Choosing the Right Model
We tested multiple models, including:
Logistic Regression (Baseline)
Random Forest
Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM)
Deep Learning (ANN with TensorFlow/Keras)
Final Choice: XGBoost performed best with 0.87 AUC-ROC, providing both interpretability and high performance.
4. Interpretability & Business Buy-in (Non-Technical Challenge)
Executives and retention teams didn’t trust AI models and wanted explainability.
Solution: We used SHAP (SHapley Additive Explanations) to show the top factors contributing to churn:
High number of support tickets
Frequent late payments
Contract nearing expiration
Lack of engagement with mobile app
This helped build trust in AI decisions.

column47:
One of the most challenging but rewarding projects I worked on was helping HDFC Bank debug a complex multi-cloud network issue. They were facing problems where traffic from AWS was being sent to a server in another cloud, but in between, there were multiple load balancers, firewalls, and other network devices (DUTs). The goal was to get everything working smoothly end-to-end and then figure out where the performance bottlenecks were.

The Challenge
The issue wasn’t straightforward because:

Too many moving parts – Traffic was flowing through multiple devices, and any one of them could have been causing the problem.
Packet drops & slow performance – The customer was seeing inconsistent failures and slowdowns, but there wasn’t a clear root cause.
Limited visibility – Since this was a production environment, we had to work with restricted access to logs and debugging tools.
Tight timelines – This was affecting banking services, so we had to solve it fast.
How We Solved It
To break it down step by step:
✅ Captured and analyzed traffic – Used tcpdump, Wireshark, and cloud monitoring tools to trace packets across the network and figure out where they were getting delayed or dropped.
✅ Systematically tested each DUT – We checked if the load balancer, firewall, or routing policies were causing issues.
✅ Found the bottleneck – After deep-dive testing, we pinpointed that the firewall couldn’t handle the high traffic volume, leading to delays.
✅ Fixed and optimized – Suggested firewall policy changes and scaling upgrades to improve performance. Also fine-tuned AWS VPC peering and routing to make sure traffic flowed seamlessly.
✅ Worked across teams – Since this involved multiple vendors and internal teams (cloud, security, and networking), I had to coordinate efforts to get things fixed efficiently.

The Outcome
🚀 Traffic started flowing smoothly end-to-end.
🚀 Performance improved by 40%, with no more unexpected slowdowns.
🚀 The debugging approach we used became a reference for future troubleshooting at HDFC Bank.

This project was a great mix of deep packet analysis, multi-cloud troubleshooting, and working under pressure. Definitely one of the most technically interesting problems I’ve tackled!

column47:
Problem:

We set out to launch a Loan Against Mutual Funds (LAMF) product with an ambitious goal: deliver a fast, intuitive D2C (direct-to-consumer) experience. However, the challenges surfaced quickly during our primary and secondary research. In India, mutual fund management typically involves the two major RTAs (Registrar and Transfer Agents)—CAMS and KFintech—both of which still rely on manual, offline procedures for critical steps like lien marking and pledging. While a few banks, such as HDFC and ICICI, have attempted to partially automate these workflows through RPAs (Robotic Process Automations), the process for determining initial eligibility and calculating the maximum loan value can still stretch over several days.

Beyond these operational hurdles, we also discovered a glaring gap in user experience: no existing LAMF solution offered a mobile-first journey. Given that most consumers rely on their smartphones for financial services, this was a significant shortcoming. Our mission was clear—streamline the offline steps, reduce the multi-day timeline, and create a truly modern, mobile-centric pathway that would dramatically improve convenience and turnaround time for customers looking to borrow against their mutual fund investments.

Solution:

After thoroughly mapping out the process, we envisioned a “north star” experience that lets users instantly pull their mutual fund holdings from RTAs, select which funds to pledge, and receive immediate—or near-immediate—loan eligibility. 

We collaborated with RTAs to develop and integrate the requisite APIs into our direct-to-consumer platform. 

The result was an industry-first LAMF journey: 65% of applicants received their credit limit within just 60 minutes of submission, and 83% secured their limit within six hours.

Enemies in games using AI : A tool to generate enemy behavior with plain text to begin with and later on dynamically fleshing out a complete story by providing a long term memory to the character something similar to the "Nemesis system".
In-Game Eco Systems: This idea is inspired from the game Rain World that contains a complete ecosystem of creatures that act based on the environments behavior and not on hard coded NPC states/actions. The creatures have been provided with a set of characteristics that lead to interesting behavior and game play. 
Life-Story Games: A service that turns a person’s photos, videos, or memories into a short, playable experience—though I know automating this with AI would require solving huge technical and ethical hurdles but it can begin with creating these playable experiences using the person's input and speeding up the asset generation,etc,etc using AI.
Learning Through Play: Simple RPG-style games where solving math puzzles or historical mysteries unlocks progress, with AI adjusting difficulty or storytelling based on the player’s pace. Another advantage of AI would be that the questions generated will be different each time so no amount of mugging up is going to help unless one really understands the concept and logically solves it.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was developing an AI-driven workflow for automating sound design and audio processing for game development. This project aimed to streamline the creation, categorization, and processing of sound effects (SFX) and music, integrating tools like Ableton Live, FFmpeg, n8n, and AI models to automate repetitive tasks and improve efficiency.

In game development, sound designers often face time-consuming and manual processes when organizing, processing, and integrating audio assets. The workflow typically involves:

Finding and curating sound effects from various sources.
Normalizing volume levels and ensuring loudness standards (LUFS).
Resampling and converting audio formats for different platforms.
Categorizing assets based on tone, mood, and usage.
Integrating processed assets into game engines.

The biggest challenge was making all of this automated and scalable, while keeping artistic control intact.

It is currently a project I would love to complete.

My professional journey has been quite a ride, a mix of academic pursuits and international experiences, and quite interesting both in person and professionally.

It all started with a Master's in Finance degree in Australia, which I followed up with four years in sales. That was where I really learned the ropes – building relationships with clients, negotiating deals, and just understanding how business works.

Then, things took an interesting turn. I had the chance to move to Kenya and work in the operations and business side of my uncle's/relative's construction and interior design business. Which was quite a learning curve! I was overseeing projects, managing teams, and trying to make everything run smoothly. It was a fantastic opportunity to develop my operational skills. Unfortunately, he passed away, and I stayed on to help manage the business through that difficult time. It was a challenging period, but I learned so much, not just about business but also about basic financial management.

After that, I came back to India to be closer to my mom and take care of her. For the last nine months, I've been dipping my toes into the world of finance. 1. One of the most challenging problems I faced was around 3-4 years back when I was interning at Tech Mahindra and they wanted to build this platform called BHAML (Bharat Markup Language). Which was a made-in-India initiative, with the mission of being a platform where people can code HTML & CSS in their regional languages.

It was one of a kind platform being built and I didn't knew much about how we would handle the problem. I was in my 2nd Year and was also the backend lead as the team was of just 5 people. We used to have meetings with the CIO, to discuss about the platform.

The problem faced was how to convert the regional language to HTML, and CSS which is being understood by the browsers. We thought of many ideas, such as training our model and mapping each tag with regional tags.
In the end, we went with mapping each tag with regional tags making sure we were following a specific structure we designed then. We didn't go ahead with the machine learning model as it wouldn't have given us 100% accuracy, and accuracy was our top priority here.

My professional journey has been a blend of curiosity-driven projects, academic exploration, and practical experience. It all started in Mumbai, where I was initially disinterested in academics and focused on personal hobbies like DIY projects, TV, and movies. However, around 7th or 8th grade, I became fascinated with building things—simple projects involving motors, batteries, and electricity sparked my interest in creating things from scratch. This curiosity continued to evolve through high school, where I built a voice-controlled car with friends for our science exhibition in 10th grade, a project that ignited my passion for hands-on building, though I didn’t grasp the deeper technical concepts at the time.

As I moved into 11th and 12th grade, my interest shifted to coding. The lockdown after 12th grade became a turning point where I immersed myself in coding, learning through projects that were both personally fulfilling and aimed at solving real-world problems. One of my notable projects was automating online college lectures, which helped me understand the power of code to solve mundane problems.

column47:
The most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on include building a voice-controlled car for my 10th standard science exhibition, automating my online college lectures during the lockdown after 12th grade, and my experience with web development during and after my 12th grade.

The Voice-Controlled Car (10th Grade):
In 10th grade, I worked with a few friends to build a voice-controlled car for our science exhibition. While I didn’t dive deeply into the technical details at the time, it was an exciting hands-on project. We used Arduino and basic components like motors and sensors to control the car’s movement using voice commands. The biggest challenge was integrating the voice control system with the car. Though I didn’t gain much technical knowledge in the process, it sparked my interest in working with hardware and coding. It was a valuable experience in building something from scratch.

The Lecture Automation (Post-12th Grade):
After 12th grade, during the lockdown, I worked on automating my online college lectures. The problem was that managing a full day of online classes was tedious, and I wanted to automate the process of attending, recording, and closing lectures.

The Problem:
The task was to automate:

Opening the Zoom lectures based on my timetable.
Starting a recording on Windows when the lecture began.
Stopping the recording and closing the lecture when it ended.
Technical Hurdles:
Learning Automation: I had to learn Python and Selenium to automate the process, which wasn’t easy since it required integrating multiple tools and making them work together.

Timing and Synchronization: The main challenge was ensuring the script synced perfectly with my timetable, starting and stopping everything at the right times. This required careful planning and testing.

Script Stability: Ensuring that the automation script worked consistently without failures for the entire day of lectures was a challenge. Debugging and improving the reliability of the script was an ongoing task.

Non-Technical Hurdles:
Time Management: Balancing this project with my studies and other responsibilities was challenging, especially since I had to spend long hours coding, debugging, and testing while also keeping up with schoolwork.

Web Development Experience:
During the same time, I also started exploring web development with a team of friends. We did a hackathon on web development and built a few websites, which sparked an interest in doing this professionally. We thought we could make money from it, so we continued learning and working on web development for about two years.

However, the biggest non-technical challenge I faced in this project was working with a team and managing client relationships. Building something technically sound is one thing, but understanding client needs and managing a team of people, each with different ideas and work styles, was something entirely different. I learned that communication is key—talking to clients to understand their needs, aligning everyone in the team towards a common goal, and managing expectations was often more difficult than the technical side of development. It was a tough lesson, but it gave me a lot of insight into how to work in teams, how to manage projects, and how to handle clients, which were important skills I took with me moving forward.

The Success:
While the voice-controlled car project sparked my initial interest in building things, the lecture automation project taught me practical coding and problem-solving skills. The web development experience helped me realize the importance of non-technical skills like communication, project management, and working with clients. In the end, all these projects contributed to my growth as a creator and helped me realize that building useful, scalable solutions is not just about technical knowledge but also about understanding how to work with people, solve real-world problems, and manage expectations.

One of the most challenging projects was during the final phase of the MVP development, just before release. The problem we faced was that the backend system at Nalli Silks had outdated data flows and business data terms, which hadn’t been updated in over 8 years. As the business grew, the system could no longer handle the increasing demands, leading to issues in inventory management, campaign optimization, and real-time data accuracy.

The plan was to address these backend issues post-MVP launch, but we realized we needed to fix them during the final UAT phase to prevent disruptions in operations post-launch.

Solution:
We decided to check the backend code line by line, thoroughly understanding all data flows. From there, we built a new backend system from scratch, with optimized data flows and updated business data terms that better aligned with the current business needs. This overhaul had to be completed in parallel with finalizing the MVP, which required intense coordination across teams.

Despite the tight timeline, we successfully integrated the new backend system just before the MVP launch, ensuring improved data accuracy, efficiency, and business alignment. The project was a success, resolving legacy issues and delivering both the MVP website and the updated backend system on time.

While working with the STES (Sinhgad Technical Education Society) Rocketry Team, where we designed and fabricated a sounding rocket targeting 10,000 feet altitude for the Spaceport America Cup competition, I identified a critical operational challenge in local manufacturing companies in Pune, which I tried to solve in my final mechanical engineering project.

While coordinating with various small-scale manufacturing units for rocket components, I observed that many of these companies operated in multi-floor facilities without elevators due to space and cost constraints. Workers were manually carrying heavy equipment and materials up and down stairs, creating safety risks and operational inefficiencies.

This observation inspired me and my friends to design and fabricate a stair-climbing robot with a 150kg load capacity. The technical challenges were substantial - we needed to create a system that could maintain stability while transitioning between steps, distribute weight effectively to prevent tipping and remain cost-effective for small businesses. Our solution incorporated a five-wheel mechanism with independent motor control and a simple stair-locking system that secured the device during climbing sequences. The most difficult hurdle was designing it to distribute weight to maintain the center of gravity within the stable region during the climbing process.

After multiple iterations and rigorous testing on various stair configurations, we successfully developed a functional prototype that could navigate standard industrial staircases while carrying heavy loads. The project received recognition from the university and local manufacturing associations, demonstrating how targeted simple engineering solutions can address practical workplace challenges in resource-constrained environments.

column47:
One of the most challenging projects I worked on was automating the generation of personalized health reports using AI and PDF generation techniques. The project aimed to integrate multiple data sources, process complex medical information, and generate structured reports for users.  

Problem : The goal was to build a dynamic health report system that could process lab test results—gut microbiome analysis, blood parameters, liver and renal function tests, and more—and present them in a well-formatted, user-friendly PDF. The key challenges included:  
- Handling diverse, large-scale medical data dynamically.  
- Ensuring accurate health insights without medical errors.  
- Overcoming limitations in client-side PDF generation.  
- Managing third-party integrations with lab data providers.  

Technical Hurdles & Approaches  
1. Dynamic Data Processing
   - The health report contained various sections, each requiring different processing logic.  
   - I structured the data using a **JSON-based schema**, ensuring flexibility and consistency in report generation.  

2. PDF Generation Issues
   - Tools like jsPDF and html2canvas struggled with rendering complex tables, images, and multi-page content.  
   - Despite optimizations (such as SVG-based rendering and performance tuning), achieving a high-quality, scalable PDF output remained a challenge.  

3. API & Data Fetching Limitations 
   - Initially, I used Google Sheets API as a temporary database, but API rate limits caused performance bottlenecks.  
   - Implemented caching strategies and optimized API calls to improve efficiency.  

Non-Technical Hurdles
1. Time & Bandwidth Constraints  
   - While the project had strong potential, I couldn’t allocate enough bandwidth to fully refine and deliver the solution.  

2. Medical Validation Complexity
   - Ensuring medical accuracy required deep domain expertise, making collaboration with healthcare professionals essential.  

Final Outcome
- While the project remained incomplete due to bandwidth constraints, it was a valuable learning experience in AI-driven document automation, healthcare data structuring, and API integration.  
- I gained hands-on exposure to real-world challenges in AI-powered PDF generation and saw the potential of automation in healthcare.  
- The experience reinforced my ability to work with complex data pipelines, optimize API interactions, and tackle product development challenges in resource-constrained environments.  

Although I wasn’t able to fully deliver the project, it remains one of the most challenging and insightful projects I’ve worked on, shaping my approach to AI-driven product development.

column47:
Project: Resolving & Predicting Metering Discrepancies Using Excel & VBA in Open Access of SER & Jharkhand Electricity Board

Problem:
Railway and State Electricity Board (SEB) meter readings are often mismatched, leading to overbilling and financial losses. Manual verification was inefficient, requiring an automated and predictive solution.

Challenges:
-Data inconsistencies between Railway and SEB readings.
-Time-consuming reconciliation & resistance from SEBs to accept errors.
-Lack of a forecasting system to detect future discrepancies.

Solution:
-Developed an Excel VBA tool to automate data comparison, highlight mismatches, and generate reports.
-Integrated trend analysis & anomaly detection to predict future errors based on historical patterns.
-Provided color-coded insights for quick decision-making and dispute resolution.

Impact:
✅ Significant cost savings by identifying and preventing overbilling.
✅ Automated & predictive bill verification, reducing manual effort.
✅ Proactive error detection ensures long-term accuracy and efficiency.

This project combined automation, data analysis, and forecasting, leading to a high-impact financial and operational solution.

I’m really excited about applying AI to the autonomous vehicle industry, and my goal is to either build a product in this space or secure a job in self-driving technology. There are several areas where AI plays a crucial role, and I’m particularly interested in AI-driven Perception Systems, where computer vision and deep learning are used for object detection, lane tracking, and sensor fusion. This is key for improving ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) and self-driving capabilities.

I’m also exploring Simulation & Virtual Testing, where AI models are trained and tested in simulated environments like CARLA and NVIDIA Drive Sim. These tools help in improving self-driving algorithms before real-world deployment. Additionally, I find Motion Planning & Control fascinating, as it involves reinforcement learning, robotics, and probabilistic models to make AI-driven decisions for navigation.

Beyond technical roles, I’m also considering AI applications in Fleet Management & Optimization, where AI agents can improve logistics and autonomous vehicle operations. Long-term, I’m open to building an AI-powered product, like self-driving delivery bots or AI-assisted driving solutions.

With 100X Engineers, I see a strong foundation being built in AI agents, automation, and reinforcement learning, which can be applied to self-driving systems. column44:
I'm passionate about solving the challenge of full autonomy in both financial markets and self-driving cars. While AI has made significant progress in these fields, neither is fully autonomous yet. In financial markets, AI-driven trading systems still require human intervention for risk management and decision-making. I want to explore how AI agents and reinforcement learning can push algorithmic trading toward greater autonomy while ensuring stability and reliability.

In the self-driving car industry, current technology is still at Level 2 or 3 autonomy, meaning human oversight is required. I’m interested in advancing AI-driven perception, motion planning, and decision-making to help achieve true Level 4 or 5 autonomy, where vehicles can operate independently in all conditions.

By combining AI agent development, reinforcement learning, and automation, I want to contribute to solving these autonomy gaps in both industries.

column17:
FREELANCING:--------------------------------------------------------------------------
I started building computer systems as a freelance gig for Private Individuals and small commercial spaces like cyber cafes and small stores. etc from around 2012 - Present.(new customers dropped heavily from around 2018, people opted for laptops/YouTube and internet assisted DIY).
I paid attention to details like customer budget, properly allocating budget to the the parts that served the primary use case, reliability of components, availability of servicing from the component brands, upgrade paths etc.

I also provided referrals to local furniture makers and stores for affordable computer/study table sets and collected referral fees.

Later I began to provide services like iPhone Jailbreaking and Android modding such as Rooting, Installing Custom ROMs. etc. These had an extremely limited customer base but I enjoyed the work.

Eventually I also began setups of small audio visual systems for Private Individuals. I also started setting up small scale networks for Private Individuals and small commercial spaces.

A good chunk of money I earned was by doing volume PC builds for cyber centres in developing areas of Bangalore.

I did all of the above with only a phone number and word of mouth from customers. No marketing apart from basic explanation on phone calls and in-person.

I would also get few customers from equipment sellers by having an agreement to only buy the equipment from their store for referred customers so they don't lose out on a potential sale. Never did commissions.

With the advent of YouTube and internet guides people's sentiment for requiring my services changed. 

I believe that a lot of people can actually build/configure a working PC or read internet reviews to choose tech products and use internet guides to setup gadgets. etc
But to provide truly excellent PCs and tailor made solutions that are optimized to customer needs one needs to put long term efforts like research discipline to find and evaluate products, hands on experience with customers, sellers, servicing industry. etc

I could not market and sell my services based on the above point due to my poor sales and marketing knowledge/experience. To supplement the reduced earnings I moved to more Traditional jobs.
__________________________________________________________________________________________


Traditional Job EXPERIENCE:-----------------------------------------------------------

2012: I did a summer job after graduating 12th.Std Pre-University, at a Dog Salon called Wags and Wiggles, learning from and helping an Entrepreneur with the ins-and-outs of bathing and grooming dogs.

2016-2017: I worked with Rangde.Org, a online Social Microfinance lending platform that provided small loans to rural people which was powered by transparent investments instead of donations from Social Investors. I helped curate social investors by engaging with potential investors through the use of Social Media(Primarily Facebook and Mail related solutions).

2017-2018: I worked as a system designer in Qubix Technologies where I designed and setup Audio Visual systems like Home Theatres ,Projector Setups. etc. Also dabbled in home automation, Got certified as an automation programmer with Control4 and automated a complete house and multiple Showrooms. Also worked with a brand called Fibaro to assist in home automation work.

2020-2021: I worked with Barely Opinionated during its early days after my neighbours started it during the COVID pandemic. It was a NEWS venture where I helped with content writing, research, Cloud Software solutions for workflows and discord community management/engagement.

2022-2025: I most recently worked at Pole To Win India as a Game Tester where we perform 3rd party Quality Assurance Testing for clients like developers and publishers in the Industry. column47:
Currently, I work at a training institute where more than 400 course/training programs are there (including retail and corporate). When I was hired, 

there two major issues: 
1. Producing a huge number of long form content for every website
2. Producing organic content for Google Ranking.

My first attempt was to solve the second issue. The content was written by researching competitors' websites and other resources. I modified this methodology. Instead of researching online, I decided to take meeting with every subject expert for an hour for personalised information regarding the questions. This data collection method provided exclusive information and personalized teaching method. This way, content stood differently. 

However, the number of content produced was yet slow. It was the time when there was a discussion whether AI content could be used for websites. At this juncture, we experimented something unique. Our thought was how the data collected from the trainers could be used for AI content generation. Will it make a change? will it make a difference? 

Technical challenges: 
The data collection methodology
how to create custom GPTs
Analysing best AI for long form content generation.
No coder to solve the issues. 

How we solved: 
Created an excel to arrange data
Hired a freelancer who is good at coding and creating custom GPTs and projects. Used all trainer data to create a project on Claude. Now every month we reach the expectation of the employer. And there is significant change in ranking too.

During the Save Soil Movement promotion in 2022, my team was responsible for driving volunteer participation on social media, primarily Twitter (now X).

In January, we started as a small group of just six members. By March, our team had grown to an incredible 4,000 volunteers. While recruitment and screening weren’t major hurdles, the real challenge lay in communication and tracking participation at such a large scale.

To streamline communication, we organized volunteers into state-wise groups of 50, ensuring manageable team sizes and fostering two-way interactions. Each group was supported by two dedicated volunteers who maintained regular contact, tracking participation and providing necessary assistance.

Although I wasn’t technically equipped, I understood the needs well enough to guide my team in developing Google Sheets-based trackers. These automated reports helped us monitor:

Volunteer participation at individual, group, and state levels.
Leaderboards and scoreboards to gamify engagement.
Activity logs of group supporters to flag any issues requiring attention.

The real test was sustaining this momentum for over four months in a purely volunteer-driven initiative with zero financial backing. With volunteers constantly joining and leaving, keeping operations smooth was no small feat—but it kept me on my toes the entire time.

We developed an MVP Electron-based application that enabled multiple users to connect via a WebRTC call. One participant could share their screen, engage in discussions over audio, browse the internet while making purchase decisions, and collaboratively draw over the shared screen with others. The most challenging and impressive project I worked on was during my internship at HEMRL, DRDO, Pune, where I conceptualized and developed a Graphical User Interface (GUI) for real-time data acquisition for blast measurements. This project was critical for defense research, as accurate blast measurements are essential for analyzing the impact of high-energy materials.

The project involved creating a system to capture and analyze real-time data from blast experiments. The existing system lacked a user-friendly interface and had limited customization options for triggering data recording. This made it difficult for researchers to efficiently analyze blast data, which is time-sensitive and requires high precision. My task was to design a GUI that could:

Acquire real-time data from pressure transducers during blast experiments.

Allow researchers to customize trigger parameters (e.g., threshold pressure levels) for data recording.

Display and store data in a structured format for post-experiment analysis.

Technical Hurdles:
1.Capturing and processing data in real-time required optimizing the system to handle high-frequency inputs without delays or data loss.

2.Ensuring the pressure transducers were accurately calibrated to measure blast pressure was challenging due to the extreme conditions of the experiments.

3.Implementing a system where users could set custom trigger thresholds required a deep understanding of both hardware and software integration.

4.Building a user-friendly GUI that could handle complex data acquisition and display it intuitively required expertise in tools like MATLAB.

Non-Technical Hurdles:
As an intern, I had to quickly learn the specific needs of researchers and defense personnel to ensure the GUI met their expectations.

How I Succeeded:
1.I began by studying the existing system and conducting discussions with researchers to understand their pain points and requirements.

2.I conducted multiple rounds of testing to refine the GUI, ensuring it was both functional and user-friendly

3.I created detailed documentation and conducted training sessions for researchers to ensure they could effectively use the new system.

The GUI I developed significantly improved the efficiency and accuracy of blast data acquisition. Researchers could now customize trigger parameters, visualize data in real-time, and store it for further analysis. The project not only enhanced the lab’s capabilities but also gave me invaluable experience in solving complex, real-world problems in a high-stakes environment.

column42:
Networks form the lifeline of the modern world and progress of businesses worldwide strongly correlates to the connectivity and stability of their underlying IT networks. IT teams that manage these networks are always operating under this pressure. It is imperative to modernize this underserved community. Today, the cost incurred in terms of complexity, network gear and personnel to maintain the status quo of network performance is significant and highly recurring and is mostly trending up.

We want to leverage advanced technology to drive a paradigm shift in this domain.    
This community needs an ecosystem that can harness global domain knowledge and have the ability to leverage the most advanced technologies in the increasingly complex landscape of networks. We want to enable IT network operations of the future, today and ensure IT teams continue to drive the lifeline of connectivity.
column44:
The problems:
Fragmentation across vendors, domains and tools
A typical enterprise network is a collection of multiple technologies spread across different networking domains. Access can be provided by wired or wireless infrastructure, sites can be connected across WANs, devices authorized based on identities or device types, flows segregated by VLANs, the list goes on. In addition, multiple networking vendors could be deployed for providing these technologies. For IT teams, this heterogeneity results in multiple tools each corresponding to a vendor or a domain. To add to this, any gaps in vendor provided tooling typically results in the development of home grown solutions that adds to the fragmentation. At this stage, the IT team is dealing with multiple vendor tools across multiple domains combined with home grown tooling needing continuous upkeep. To exacerbate the problem, vendors have their own release cycles and any new features typically take years to get rolled out at scale. In this suboptimal scenario, continuous operational efficiency is far from feasible and the IT team is simply making do with this fragmented reality. This environment holds IT teams back and operating the network as a ‘whole’, across all domains with one unified approach remains a distant dream. This fragile approach is a risk to the underlying business both operationally as well as in terms of security.

Operational inefficiencies from lack of continuous insightful visibility
Driving efficient network operations is a continuous process that needs uninterrupted consumption and processing of telemetry for real time visibility. IT networks are complex systems with numerous entities functioning in parallel having multi-dimensional inter-dependencies. These entities can be physical or logical in nature, such as, wireless or switching mediums, networking hardware, end-user devices, protocols and even the  applications being served over the network. Every entity introduces their own behavioral characteristics in the network and generates its own telemetry. Telemetry volume scales exponentially with the count of entities in the network and visibility alone using this telemetry quickly becomes counterproductive leading to information overload. With IT teams getting leaner and asked to do more with less, having access to visibility alone does not reduce the burden. IT teams lack access to continuous operational insights capturing the most relevant information, historical behavioral patterns, the most active areas of the network, best practices, peer comparisons and more. In addition there is a severe lack of early warning systems to prevent or notify IT teams before potentially disrupting network events take place. This needs a radical approach that current tooling in the industry fails to provide as they are not built with data as the foundational construct.

Increasing skill gap and decreasing team size
Over the past years, personnel talent in core networking technologies has been on the decline and there is a significant churn observed in enterprise IT teams (captured here,  here and here). This is a concerning trend that doesn't have a foreseeable inflection point and has long term ramifications. As networks continue to get complex and reliance on it for business success only increases, lean team sizes and shortage of experienced personnel poses significant risk to growth. IT teams are simply not staffed to manage multi-dimensional network operations and in smaller enterprises, less experienced personnel run the risk of not being aware of impending disruptions or security incidents. IT teams lack an intelligent ecosystem where best practices are recommended, gaps are identified, remediations are proposed with automated deployment of changes. In addition there is a lack of a community based, data driven exchange of tools, best practices, knowledge sharing and deployment methodologies. In the absence of an ecosystem, IT teams operate in the silo of their own business. Inexperience in this domain can lead to decisions that are suboptimal and detrimental in the long run when there are no knowledge-based driven guardrails in place to prevent it.
column45:
Taking a data first approach and abstracting a network into high fidelity logical data entities allows the platform to capture complex inter-dependencies, properties and characteristics between various functions and network components. This approach allows us to apply principles of AI from the ground up. This means graph technologies, baselining methodologies, anomaly detection, prediction mechanisms are natively built into the platform and can be applied on the telemetry collected. MLOps capabilities are natively built into the platform to support AI/ML models being trained and deployed and applied at scale across the networking stack. Generative AI methodologies to interact with the data, interact with the AI generated insights are built into the platform, creating many modes of interacting with the platform.

Every part of the network is not equally critical and as such needs differentiated focus. IT teams need to ensure the most critical and heavily used sections of the network are fully operational and traditionally give priority of their attention to those areas. This fundamental change in approaching visibility is missing today. Visibility into the network and insights into underlying performance cannot be applied in a ‘one size fits all’ approach that almost all vendor tools today do. Sophistication and intelligence is required to determine critical sections of the network, network areas that are heavily relied on, and key behaviors that need to be tracked for these areas. Using an ensemble of techniques including Machine Learning and Deep Learning, these characteristics can be determined and specifically tracked closely providing higher visibility into potential upcoming issues or suboptimal performance. Also, as the network is abstracted into data entities with relationships, a multitude of approaches based on graph neural networks can be applied to determine dependencies, change in trends, predictions based on past behavior. These relationships can be complex and multi-dimensional and cannot always be foreseen or encoded into tools by IT teams, for example: how does a power supply of a switch impact the wireless connectivity of users in a building many hops away. This platform will be providing continuous insights, intelligence and relevant visibility that is operationally significant for the IT team.

Network operators have always suffered from the inability to extend vendor provided tools with their own operating metrics and custom analytics. The existing solutions from vendors have large feature release cycles and are often late to extend key features. We aim to leverage the common data platform to provide an analytics toolkit where customers can design their own custom analytics and make them operational on the platform. This will allow customers to build complete workflows end to end from data collection across different sources, to visualization and analytics on the system. These analytics workflows will be available on a marketplace where other customers of the platform can integrate it into their instance of the product. This is especially powerful since these workflows will be built across vendors and will allow a community driven intelligence platform. This will truly enable the operator community to leverage decades of cumulative expertise from each other and move faster than the slow feature lifecycles that vendors typically provide. Customers that are not able to build large IT teams will especially benefit from this whereas those with large IT teams will operate better. In addition to this, teams that have a skill gap and are low on experience will be able to leverage the expertise built on this platform from more mature teams.
column47:
Research and development of Enterprise Networking usecases driven by AI/ML needs was a platform or infrastructure that would allow data ingestion, compute and AI/ML model development and iteration at large scale. The fundamental building block of Networking AI/ML solutions is the gathering of network telemetry across the networking stack at scale: end user and network devices. For end users, this could be the collection of the full range of user experience: ranging from real user traffic to synthetic test data for services and on-device flow data. For network devices, this could be wired telemetry ranging from device characteristics like CPU, memory, and environment, to network telemetry like interface statistics, routing tables, queue stats, client states etc. Wireless telemetry would capture characteristics like interference, RSSI, onboarding times, client roaming details and much more. Similar to this, SDWAN telemetry captures tunnel behavior, routing paths, SaaS utilization, client stats and similar metrics.

Gathering, storing and accessing this data was a very difficult problem to solve as telemetry from the network stack is generated at Big Data scale requiring a paradigm shift in data collection and storage approaches, followed by a completely different methodology for data access and isolation to compute operations at scale across multiple engineers. In addition to this, there was an absence of a mature AI/ML platform that allows teams from different groups to carry out research in this space, iterate over large amounts of data, build and train AI/ML models at scale. This severely impeded any innovation, development or exploration in this space. A platform like this offers not only an interface to data exploration, but also an integrated MLOps framework to build AI/ML models, store them in a model registry and serve them at scale. With data collection and data storage come the responsibility of data governance, data privacy and security not to mention geographical aspects such as GDPR. Such an operational framework to manage these aspects of customer data at scale had not been put in place through an operational customer facing platform.

I was a key architect and lead engineering lead in 2016 that started the effort to build a Network Analytics product for Cisco enterprise networking. In order to build this product, we had to first build a cloud platform and so we started the development of product and the cloud platform in parallel. This was a revolutionary approach back then, especially when enterprise network operators did not want to send telemetry to the cloud and AI/ML was not pervasive. There was a completely new architecture that needed to be build that would securely export network telemetry from on-prem to the cloud and then send intelligence back to on-prem networking devices. This eventually culminated in the launch of Cisco AI Network Analytics in 2019 which is still one of a kind product for AI/ML application to Enterprise Networking.

Eventually I became the Director of Engineering to lead this team during its development. The success of the platform and the application we built on it was the result of intense collaboration between talented engineers that spanned 5 countries and three timzones with multi-domain specialization spanning Cloud Infrastructure, Data Technologies, AI/ML Methodologies and Networking expertise. Wearing multiple technical and leadership hats, we worked closely with various functions within Cisco: Wireless and Switching teams, Data Governance, Security and Trust, Cloud Operations, Finance as well as AWS Technology Groups for the managed services. These highly cross functional engagements were challenging and at the same time necessary for the success of the launch. For many of these cross-functional engagements, this was also the first time such a platform was being released in Cisco. This has been one of my most challenging projects as there were many technical, non-technical and organizational 'firsts' that we were attempting at the same time. column17:
After completing my B.Tech in Food Technology from Amity University Noida in 2018, I ventured into entrepreneurship by co-founding two restaurant brands, Bistro B2 (a casual dining restaurant) and Basant Bahar Delight (a QSR) in Varanasi with my brother. With no prior experience in hospitality, we faced operational and leadership challenges, leading to inefficiencies. In 2020, after two years of struggling to optimize operations, I decided to exit the restaurant business completely, and my brother took over.

I then joined my family’s 60-year-old Indian sweets and namkeen business, focusing on modernizing operations by implementing a POS system, production planning, inventory management, standardized recipes, and process audits. While these initiatives initially improved efficiency, sustaining them was difficult due to limited resources and leadership conflicts. In April 2024, I decided to step away from the family business as it no longer aligned with my long-term goals.

In April 2022, my brother exited the restaurant business, and I took over operations independently. With full control, I restructured and optimized operations, implemented technology solutions such as kitchen display screens, mobile ordering systems, inventory management, and financial audits, and focused on building a strong workplace culture to improve service quality and team efficiency. Today, the restaurant runs successfully without my daily involvement.

From May 2024 to December 2024, I worked pro bono with a YouTube channel (The Boho Sapiens, 6.9 lakh subscribers) to streamline their operations. My role involved building SOPs for different parts of the content creation process to ensure consistency and efficiency, researching and integrating new AI tools to enhance workflow automation, and playing a key role in hiring and team building to scale content production. I also worked on researching and introducing a structured scriptwriting framework that significantly improved the performance of long-form content, developing custom GPTs to automate the scriptwriting process, and building content strategies based on analytics data to optimize engagement and growth. Additionally, I focused on researching appropriate learning resources to help the team upskill and improve content quality.

Throughout this journey, I have grown significantly—learning to navigate challenges, build structured systems, leverage technology for automation, and lead teams effectively. These experiences have shaped my perspective on business, leadership, and adaptability, preparing me for my next phase of growth.

I aim to build strong foundational skills in AI that will serve me long-term. My primary goal is to learn a structured approach to AI product development by gaining hands-on experience in the technical, market, and business aspects of AI. This will enable me to explore various opportunities in the AI space and make well-informed decisions about my direction.

While practical application is crucial, I also want to develop a strong grasp of AI fundamentals necessary for making informed business decisions. A solid understanding of core AI concepts  will help me assess opportunities, evaluate risks, and determine the feasibility of AI-driven solutions. Given time constraints, I would appreciate recommendations for supplementary resources to explore advanced concepts independently.

Additionally, I aim to build a foundational understanding of coding to effectively leverage AI coding assistants and AI-powered development tools. Understanding coding fundamentals will allow me to use these tools more efficiently, rather than relying on them as black-box solutions. At the same time, I want to explore the capabilities and limitations of no-code tools to determine where coding becomes essential. This will help me make informed decisions about tool selection, integration, and scalability.

As I gain hands-on experience, I also seek to develop a practical understanding of software development processes. This will enhance my ability to communicate effectively with developers, assess project complexity, and estimate time and effort for implementation—crucial skills for setting realistic expectations and ensuring smooth collaboration in future AI projects.

While I am new to the technical side of AI, my experience in running multiple small-scale businesses has given me a deep understanding of operational challenges and the complexities of implementing technology in business workflows. I am passionate about optimizing processes, leveraging automation, and bridging the gap between AI technology and real-world applications.

Recognizing the need to strengthen my technical expertise, I am eager to learn from the cohort while contributing my business and operational insights. I look forward to engaging with mentors and peers to accelerate my learning and collaborate on impactful projects.

While my primary focus is learning and experimentation, I hope that by the end of the cohort, I can monetize the product or service I develop, making it self-sustainable and helping fund my further learning and exploration. Though not my priority, this would be an added advantage.

I am excited about this journey and eager to immerse myself in both the technical and strategic aspects of AI, ensuring I make well-informed decisions about my future path while building impactful AI-driven solutions.

I plan to build B2B AI SaaS solutions, but before fully committing to a scalable SaaS product, I intend to start with AI automation services for businesses where I have firsthand experience. Having run a restaurant, Indian sweets & namkeen manufacturing, and retail business, and having worked on a YouTube channel, I am well aware of the operational challenges in these industries. Additionally, I have access to businesses in education (school), finance (CA firm), and legal services (law firm), which I can explore as I gain more experience.

My Approach:

- I will begin by developing AI agents to automate processes in my own businesses, allowing me to experiment, iterate, and fail in a controlled environment without external pressure. This hands-on experience will not only deepen my understanding of AI applications but also help me self-fund my learning and experimentation.  
- By sharing my journey publicly, I aim to attract potential clients for AI automation services.  
- As I identify repeated client needs and scalable automation opportunities, I will transition toward building a full-fledged AI SaaS product.  

Why Start with an AI Automation Agency First?  

1. Lack of Technical Expertise – I need to develop foundational AI and coding skills before diving into SaaS.  
2. Learn Through Experimentation – This approach allows me to test and refine ideas without large-scale risk.  
3. Self-Funding – My existing businesses can help cover the costs of early development.  
4. Limited Access to Funding – Without prior AI experience, securing external investment would be challenging.  
5. Building Credibility & Proof of Concept – Successful projects will provide case studies, testimonials, and a strong portfolio to validate future SaaS offerings.  
6. Team Building – Initially, attracting top AI talent will be difficult without a proven track record.  

When Would I Pivot to SaaS?  

I will transition to building a scalable AI SaaS product when:  

1. Credibility – Successful delivery of automation projects demonstrates market demand and my ability to execute.  
2. Technical Expertise – I have gained the necessary skills or built a capable team.  
3. Financial Readiness – The automation agency generates enough cash flow to support SaaS development.  
4. Scalable Market Opportunity – While solving problems for my businesses, I will study market demand and pivot if I identify a repeatable and scalable solution.  
5. Client Demand Patterns – A common problem/request emerges, signaling an opportunity for a standardized SaaS product.  
6. Operational Readiness – The automation service is streamlined and systemized, making it easier to transition into a product-based business.

In today’s world, many people struggle to find fulfillment in their work due to rigid structures that prioritize efficiency over individual creativity and passion. While AI-driven automation may replace many jobs, it also has the potential to create new opportunities, allowing people to explore their true interests. However, this transition must be carefully managed to avoid major economic and social challenges.

Every individual has unique potential, but traditional work structures often limit exploration by enforcing predefined career paths. While some thrive in these systems, many do not, leading to untapped human potential on a large scale. AI is not just replacing repetitive tasks; as reasoning models improve, even problem-solving jobs will be automated. In this new reality, intent and purpose will become far more important than execution since AI will handle much of the operational workload. The key challenge is ensuring that this shift benefits everyone and does not worsen inequalities.

One of the issues I am most passionate about is improving mental health. As AI reduces the need for human labor, people will have more opportunities to engage in fulfilling activities and build communities based on shared interests and values. However, mental health must be prioritized to help individuals navigate these changes effectively.

The concept of the Network State, as described by Balaji Srinivasan, excites me because it presents a vision where like-minded communities can self-organize, driven by shared goals and values. I see potential in this framework for fostering environments that promote mental well-being on a large scale. While I recognize that this vision must be over-optimistic, I believe in taking a proactive approach—working towards a better future rather than falling into a negative mindset.

Mental health is one of the most fundamental aspects of human well-being. While it is not the only cause of large-scale societal challenges, improving mental health could lead to more thoughtful, compassionate, and solution-oriented individuals who can collectively address broader social and economic issues. My goal is to contribute to initiatives that enhance mental well-being, enabling people to make the most of the opportunities that AI and automation will bring.
