The Most Challenging and Impressive Project: Restaurant Takeover and Turnaround

Background & Context

• In 2018, I co-founded a restaurant business with my brother. After initial struggles, leadership conflicts arose due to differing management styles. Following the first wave of COVID-19 in 2020, we mutually decided that my brother would run the restaurant alone while I joined my family business of manufacturing and retail of Indian sweets.

• By 2022, my brother wanted to exit the restaurant business as it was making significant losses and was no longer financially viable. Instead of shutting it down, I saw this as an opportunity to turn it around and prove myself. However, this meant taking over a failing business while simultaneously managing my family business.

• At the time of takeover (April 2022), the restaurant was in a severe financial crisis with negative cash flow—employees’ salaries were delayed, and vendors refused to supply on credit.

• Despite these challenges, I committed to making the restaurant profitable. My approach was to identify fundamental operational inefficiencies, restructure processes, and invest strategically to achieve profitability as soon as possible.

Challenges & Solutions

1. Overcoming Financial and Emotional Pressure
   • The biggest challenge in a cash-negative business is the constant pressure from creditors—employees demanding salaries, vendors refusing supplies, and government dues accumulating.
   • Instead of resorting to short-term fixes like borrowing more money to cover existing debts, I focused on improving cash flow through operational efficiency and prioritizing payments strategically.
   • First step: I borrowed money from my mother and immediately cleared pending employee salaries. This was a strategic decision because gaining employees’ trust was critical to implementing operational changes successfully.
   • To manage emotions, I made a conscious decision to stay patient and focus on long-term solutions rather than reacting impulsively to external pressures.

2. Operational Audit & Identifying Root Causes
   • I assigned an employee whom I was training under me from the past two years in my family business to conduct a week-long detailed operational audit, observing processes from opening to closing.
   • I also conducted one-on-one meetings with every employee to understand internal challenges. The major issues identified were:
     - Inefficient service time (dine-in customers were dissatisfied with delays).
     - Daily cash-based inventory purchases due to vendor credit refusal, leading to supply chain issues and high costs.
     - Toxic work culture—politics, lack of motivation, and high employee turnover.
     - Extreme cost-cutting, negatively impacting quality.
     - Lack of systems & processes—employees were constantly firefighting rather than planning, creating the illusion of staff shortage.
     - Security loopholes, increasing the risk of theft and leakages.
   • Rather than getting overwhelmed, I applied the 80/20 principle, focusing on one high-impact project at a time, starting with service time improvement.

3. Improving Service Time (First Major Operational Fix)

   Problem:
   • Restaurant layout issue: The kitchen was on the 2nd floor, while dine-in areas were on the ground and first floors, causing excessive staff movement.
   • Role confusion: Staff members had undefined responsibilities, leading to inefficiencies.
   • Manual order-taking process: Senior stewards took orders manually, relayed them to the cashier, and junior stewards delivered them to the kitchen.
   • Online order handling issues: Delivery personnel crowded the main cash counter, creating bottlenecks.
   • Lack of structured task allocation: Basic tasks like table setup and utensil wiping were not done during lean hours, further slowing service time.

   Solutions Implemented:
   A) Technology Integration
      • Kitchen Display System (KDS): Orders were now directly displayed on kitchen screens, eliminating the need for stewards to run with KOTs (Kitchen Order Tickets).
      • Captain Ordering App: Senior stewards now placed orders digitally from their mobile phones, eliminating manual order-taking delays.
      • Walkie-Talkies for seamless communication between kitchen staff and stewards.

   B) Role Optimization & Task Specialization
      • Online order pickup point shifted: Delivery riders now collected orders from a dedicated back entrance near the kitchen, eliminating unnecessary front-counter congestion.
      • Junior stewards assigned specialized roles:
        - One steward exclusively managed KDS in the kitchen.
        - One steward handled dine-in food pickup (no more waiting in the kitchen).
        - One steward focused solely on online order packaging & dispatch.
      • Lean-hour utilization: Tables were pre-set, and utensils were cleaned before peak hours, reducing last-minute delays.

   Results:
   • Service time reduced from 35-40 minutes to under 20 minutes during peak hours.
   • Staff no longer felt overwhelmed, and attrition rates decreased.
   • Customer satisfaction improved, leading to increased online orders (which are highly sensitive to service speed).

4. Inventory & Vendor Management

   Problem:
   • Vendors refused bulk supply on credit, forcing daily cash purchases, increasing procurement costs, and causing frequent stock shortages.
   • Delays in ingredient availability further affected kitchen efficiency.

   Solution:
   • Negotiated weekly advance payments with vendors instead of daily purchases.
   • Explained the business situation transparently, securing vendor commitment by ensuring them regular bulk orders in exchange for deferred pending payments.
   • Impact: Reduced procurement costs, ensured timely stock availability, and minimized operational disruptions.

5. Developing a Robust Audit & Performance System

   Problem:
   • Past attempts to rely on managers to enforce discipline had failed.
   • No structured monitoring system existed to track who was following processes and who wasn’t.

   Solution:
   • Created a detailed Operations Manual, including:
     - SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) for each process.
     - Employee responsibility charts, ensuring clear role definitions.
     - Daily & weekly checklists for all tasks.
     - A structured audit system with a dedicated auditor monitoring compliance both physically and via CCTV.
   • Introduced performance-based incentives instead of penalties to boost employee motivation.
   • Focused on regular meetings to change employee mindset, helping them see long-term benefits of structured work.

   Result:
   • Employees became more accountable, and operational efficiency improved.
   • Restaurant achieved operational break-even in just 5 months.

Final Impact & Key Lessons Learned

• Taking over a failing restaurant and turning it around was one of the most challenging yet rewarding experiences of my career. It reinforced several critical business lessons that continue to shape my approach:

1. Emotional Resilience in High-Stress Situations
   • The biggest challenge wasn’t just fixing operations—it was maintaining patience, confidence, and clarity while dealing with relentless financial pressure. Learning to stay composed despite external stressors was crucial in executing long-term solutions effectively.

2. Structured Execution Over Impulsive Cost-Cutting
   • Many struggling businesses resort to short-term cost-cutting as a survival instinct, but this often worsens the problem. Instead, focusing on systematic improvements and long-term efficiencies led to a sustainable turnaround.

3. Investing in People and Systems Instead of Chasing Quick Fixes
   • The restaurant’s revival wasn’t just about operational tweaks; it was about building trust with employees, improving work culture, and implementing robust systems. Empowering people and creating structured workflows led to lasting efficiency gains, whereas quick-fix solutions would have only delayed the inevitable.

4. Execution Is the Hardest Part
   • The solutions I implemented—improving service time, vendor management, and employee performance—were not complex or revolutionary. The real challenge was executing them patiently and strategically in an environment of financial uncertainty, ensuring that every change was both sustainable and scalable.

Future Vision & Next Steps

• With the restaurant now stable and profitable, my focus has shifted to the next phase of optimization. I strongly believe that AI-driven automation can further streamline operations, reduce human dependencies, and enhance efficiency. My goal is to explore AI-powered solutions that can eventually replace my operational involvement, making the business even more autonomous and scalable.

• This experience not only strengthened my ability to manage and scale businesses but also reinforced my belief in leveraging technology to optimize traditional industries. Now, as I transition into AI, I see immense potential in combining structured execution with cutting-edge AI-driven automation.

column47:
one of the things i’ve done that i’m most proud of is helping my family with their first hospital. my parents are doctors, and we moved to raipur, chhattisgarh in 2015, where they had to build everything from the ground up—no established name, no legacy, and no existing patient base. the healthcare sector was booming, and raipur was becoming a medical hub for nearby districts and state, with new hospitals opening almost every week in raipur. they launched their hospital in december 2021, but despite the growing number of medical tourists from nearby states like odisha, jharkhand, and chhattisgarh, our hospital wasn’t doing well.

i wanted to understand why, so i started talking to the patients who did come in. i asked them how they found us, and most said they came because their first-choice doctor—a well-known name—was unavailable. that’s when i realized something. people who traveled long distances from odisha, jharkhand, and rural chhattisgarh for treatment only visited hospitals where they felt familiar with the doctor, either through legacy, marketing, word of mouth, or reputation. a doctor’s name and reputation played a crucial role in attracting patients. most patients who visited us only did so because their preferred doctor was unavailable.

i discussed this with my parents and suggested a solution—conducting free medical camps in villages and small towns across nearby states and districts. these camps offered free consultations and basic diagnostics, allowing potential patients to meet my parents, build trust, and become familiar with our hospital. over time, my parents organized around 20 medical camps, significantly increasing awareness. as a result, more medical tourists recognized our hospital, and we saw a substantial increase in patient visits.

this experience taught me the importance of trust in healthcare, and seeing how a simple insight transformed my family’s hospital is something i will always be proud of.

column47:
When I was a freelancer & had a small team, we used to manage our funds through Payoneer & in 2021, one day out of nowhere I just received an email that my account was blacked with no reason (you violated a policy & no mention of which one).

That Payoneer account had a lot of money in it (my small freelance team's next 6 months' emergency funds + our marketing budget for the next 8 months).

This incident shook my belief system; all my plans shattered. I stopped working on my business, my team, my clients, 

and I just went into the legal hole of things to win my case & get the money back, and then eventually, after 2 months of constant panic & stress & seeing my company fall (because I was just unable to focus on anything else), finally we got the decision that it will take them 180 days to release. Even after that, instead of just focusing on the growth part of my agency, I started saving the ship with the hope that I just had to survive 4 more months.

Eventually, after 6 months, they said it is locked for an indefinite time & again with no proper answer.

For Another 3 months, I took the legal battle into my own hands, learned almost everything through myself & my research, and couldn't afford a lawyer in the US.

Just like that, 1+ year went by.

That was hard to swallow, really shook my beliefs; my partner left me with the entire team hanging.

Then, I started with the foundation again, I let my ego not drive me & started working on small advertising accounts myself, started putting in the hours, focused on my strengths & started rebuilding the team this time with processes, proper KPI's so that even if I am not there, I am not the agency driver.

Maybe it was a shift from a freelancer mindset to an agency mindset. That was my learning, taught me humility & showed me the importance of family, how they will stick by you & when everyone will blame you for your actions.

Sometimes, I am glad it happened, because at that time—even though I was winning easily & making money , I was actually at the peak of "Mount Stupid" as per the Dunning-Kruger effect.

Lastly, there were many challenges I solved for my clients or brands I worked with, but this is the problem that really made me a different person, to go start learning again in life & never stop learning & maybe taught me the first hard lesson of entrepreneurship.

column47:
The Challenge:
We initially launched the product as a B2C solution, but despite investing $36,000 in marketing, we only sold 278 out of 2,500 devices, signaling a lack of product-market fit. Additionally, we faced challenges such as:

Low trust in AI-powered tracking—parents were hesitant to use a device tracking their children.
Limited early adoption—the product appealed only to tech-savvy users.
Manufacturing & distribution hurdles—our hardware was sourced from Foxconn, which required high minimum order quantities (MOQ).
Team conflicts—co-founders wanted to raise VC funding, but I believed we needed to pivot first.
The Solution & Execution:
Instead of scaling inefficiently, I took a first-principles approach and reassessed the market:
1️⃣ Pivoted to a B2B Model – Partnered with schools to sell devices in bulk, adding their branding & safety endorsements to build trust.
2️⃣ Addressed Consumer Concerns –

Introduced health certifications to ease concerns over radiation risks.
Created a "Better Safe Than Sorry" awareness campaign in parent-teacher meetings.
3️⃣ Improved AI Features – Integrated Smart Safe Zones using ML to dynamically adjust based on a child’s movement patterns.
4️⃣ Optimized Distribution & Pricing – Implemented a tiered pricing model for schools vs. parents, ensuring affordability and scalability.
The Outcome:
🚀 Sold 2,500 devices 
Partnered with 15 pre-schools 

Key Takeaways:
A great product isn’t enough—market trust and the right distribution strategy are crucial.
Listening to users and pivoting early can save a product from failure.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was co-founding a platform that connects homebuyers to down payment assistance programs and affordable mortgage financing. The goal was to simplify access to financial resources and help middle-income individuals achieve homeownership.

Problem Statement:

Despite billions of dollars in grants available for homebuyers, the process of accessing them is fragmented, complex, and highly inefficient. Individuals often struggle to find the right programs, navigate eligibility criteria, and complete the necessary paperwork. Financial institutions and brokers also lacked a centralized system to connect qualified buyers with available funding.

Challenges Faced:

Technical Challenges:
	1.	Data Aggregation & Structuring – The biggest technical hurdle was consolidating information from 25,000+ down payment assistance programs across the U.S. into a structured and searchable database.
	2.	Automating Eligibility Matching – Creating an algorithm that could match homebuyers with the right grants based on factors like income, credit score, and location.
	3.	Website UX & Scalability – Ensuring a seamless user experience while handling thousands of queries efficiently.

Non-Technical Challenges:
	1.	Building Credibility & Trust – Since financial services require high trust, we had to establish partnerships with lenders, brokers, and financial agencies to gain legitimacy.
	2.	User Acquisition & Market Education – Many homebuyers weren’t even aware that these programs existed, so marketing and educating users became a key challenge.
	3.	Funding & Monetization – Figuring out how to generate sustainable revenue while keeping the service accessible to the target audience.

How We Succeeded:

was a co-founder of an Animation Institute, and it was one of the most challenging projects I've ever been part of. We started completely from scratch—everything from building our brand identity to generating leads. I was in charge of the lead generation strategy and had a small team of three to guide me through the process.

One key idea we landed on was targeting students who had taken supplementary exams. We figured these students were really into the creative field and would be the perfect fit for what we were offering. On the technical side, setting up and fine-tuning our digital marketing campaigns was a steep learning curve. I had to experiment with different strategies to reach our ideal audience effectively.

On the non-technical front, the biggest hurdle was creating a brand story that resonated with our audience. We had to build trust from the ground up and convince these students that our institute could truly help them succeed in a competitive creative industry.

In the end, our combined efforts in strategic marketing, strong team collaboration, and a clear focus on our target audience helped us succeed in establishing a strong brand presence. This experience taught me a lot about resilience, teamwork, and the power of a well-thought-out strategy.

column47:
The most complex and impactful project I’ve worked on was Campaign Planner at Twitter. The core problem we tackled was helping new advertisers determine how much budget to allocate and what ROI they could expect when advertising on Twitter. Many advertisers, whether small businesses or large agencies, lacked clarity on how much money they needed to spend to achieve a certain number of impressions or clicks. Typically, they would experiment with small budgets and slowly scale up, leading to inefficiencies. The goal of Campaign Planner was to provide them with a tool that could offer accurate budget recommendations upfront, thereby increasing advertiser confidence and engagement from the start.

Since there was no established way to solve this problem, I had to start from scratch. The first step was scoping down the problem to make it manageable. Instead of tackling all ad formats, I focused initially on reach-based ads, which are billed on a cost-per-impression basis and are the most fundamental type of ads on Twitter.

Next, I had to identify relevant datasets within Twitter to help predict ad performance based on targeting criteria. This required extensive data exploration and feature engineering to understand key factors influencing impressions, reach, and cost per impression. Additionally, there was a technical challenge in accessing audience size data—this data existed but was refreshed periodically, and I needed a way to integrate it efficiently without reinventing the wheel. I collaborated with another team to prioritize access to their API, which provided real-time audience size estimates and became a key input to my model.

My initial approach was to frame this as a point prediction problem, where the model would predict impressions and reach based on budget and targeting criteria. In offline evaluations, the model performed well. However, I noticed nonsensical trends in some predictions—for instance, there were cases where increasing the budget didn’t necessarily lead to higher impressions, which is counterintuitive.

To solve this, I reformulated the problem from a point prediction model to a Pareto curve prediction model. Instead of predicting single-point estimates, the model would output a distribution of possible outcomes, ensuring logical and consistent trends in budget-to-impression relationships.

This was my first project at Twitter, and I was the only data scientist on my team, which meant I had to navigate multiple challenges beyond just modeling. Since our team had never deployed an ML model before, I had to work with another data science team to push it to production. Convincing them of the model’s validity required detailed explanations, multiple rounds of discussions, and refinements. After several iterations and stakeholder buy-in, we successfully launched Campaign Planner.

The tool was widely adopted by advertisers, significantly improving budget allocation decisions. It was well received, leading to a published Twitter blog post, and became an integral part of the Twitter Ads ecosystem. Seeing advertisers leverage AI-driven insights to make better ad spend decisions was incredibly rewarding, and it solidified my interest in building AI solutions that drive real business impact.

This process helped us identify instances of tax evasion that resulted in tangible returns to the government through fines.
